filters.predicate: (l_shipdate >= 8766 AND l_shipdate < 9131) AND (l_discount >= 5 AND l_discount <= 7) AND l_quantity < 2400

scan.access_pattern: sequential shipdate scan per thread, then monotonic sparse probes into discount/quantity and finally extendedprice for compacted survivor row ids

data_structures.fact_join: thread-local row_id vector for shipdate candidates, then thread-local survivor row_id vector plus compact discount payload; no join

parallelism.threads: auto(nproc) with static contiguous row partitions and thread-local compaction/reduction

indexes.purpose: precheck candidate density only; bypass block-pruning path when all 600 blocks overlap
precheck candidate density only; do not drive the hot loop when overlap is dense
precheck candidate density only; do not drive the hot loop when overlap is dense

optimization_notes: Root cause: the current fused scan does work that is badly out of proportion to the output, streaming about 1.60 GiB per execution and reading `l_extendedprice` for 97.6% of rows that fail the predicates, while also paying to touch `l_discount` and `l_quantity` for 83.9% of rows rejected by the cheap 4-byte `l_shipdate` filter. Strategy Category A/C: reorder the scan to filter on `l_shipdate` first, compact qualifying row ids in thread-local vectors, probe `l_discount` and `l_quantity` only for those monotonically increasing candidates, compact final survivors with their small discount payload, and late-materialize `l_extendedprice` only for the final survivor list before reducing into thread-local sums. Keep the three 600-entry zone maps only as a density precheck and bypass block-pruning on this hot path, avoiding the prior dense-bitmap and tiny-tile regressions while cutting expected bytes touched to roughly 1.00 GiB.
